aliases: Beef Salad With Lime (Gỏi Bò Tái Chanh)

english: Beef Salad With Lime (Gỏi Bò Tái Chanh)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese lime beef salad; mapped to the broader 'beef dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a dedicated code for Gỏi Bò Tái Chanh; A0CKP (beef salad) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific Vietnamese beef salad preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Gỏi Bò Tái Chanh is approximately 200 g, representing one plate as commonly served in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Salad With Lime (Gỏi Bò Tái Chanh) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Salad With Lime (Gỏi Bò Tái Chanh), cr_id G00010574. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gỏi Bò Tái Chanh. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on analogous mixed salad with protein and low-carbohydrate dressing from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains only 5.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from lime juice and a small amount of sugar in the dressing. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 11.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.8, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg per 100 g from fish sauce dressing, exceeding the 600 mg/100 g threshold; potassium at 64 mg/100 g is insufficient to offset the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish unless prepared with significantly reduced fish sauce; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 1620 mg sodium, representing 70–81% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is low at 5.9 g/100 g with sugar at 4.69 g/100 g, supporting glycaemic control; however, fat is very high at 44.54 g/100 g, which may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is low in carbohydrate and has a low glycaemic index (~32), making it suitable for blood glucose management in moderate portions, but the high fat content (predominantly from peanuts and dressing oil) warrants portion control and monitoring of total dietary fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is 44.54 g/100 g and sodium is 810 mg/100 g; the combination of high dietary fat, high sodium, and cholesterol at 26 mg/100 g presents a compounded cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its high total fat and sodium content; if consumed, portion size should not exceed 100 g and fish sauce should be requested at reduced quantity to lower sodium exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; sodium at 810 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue oedema; chili (nightshade) may trigger joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly limit or avoid this dish; the red meat base provides pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid, and the high-sodium fish sauce dressing exacerbates synovial inflammation and fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gỏi Bò Tái Chanh (Beef Salad with Lime) is a classic Vietnamese cold salad dish featuring thinly sliced beef that is either briefly cooked or lime-cured (tái chanh), giving the meat a tender, slightly firm texture with a bright citrus flavour. The dish is dressed with a pungent fish sauce and lime juice vinaigrette, typically sweetened with a small amount of sugar and sharpened with fresh chili and garlic. It is garnished with fried shallots, roasted peanuts, and an abundance of fresh Vietnamese herbs including mint (rau húng), perilla (tía tô), and coriander (ngò rí), which contribute aromatic complexity and antioxidant phytochemicals. Per 100 g, the dish provides 430 kcal, 44.54 g fat, 1.32 g protein, and 5.9 g carbohydrate, reflecting the dominant caloric contribution of peanuts and dressing oil rather than the beef itself. Sodium is high at 810 mg/100 g due to the fish sauce base. The dish is low in carbohydrate with a low estimated glycaemic index (~32), making it nominally suitable for blood glucose management, but the high fat and sodium content raise concerns for cardiovascular and hypertensive patients. It is commonly served as a starter or light main course at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore and is associated with lunch and dinner occasions. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains fish (fish sauce) and peanut allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010574), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. The record is marked as reviewed with confidence 0.95 in the source system. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this dish.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (G00010574) and are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent Vietnamese lime beef salad preparation as of 2026. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB source). protein_g is 1.32 g/100 g, which is low but reflects the dish composition where peanuts and dressing oil dominate the caloric profile and the beef content per 100 g is modest relative to the dressing volume; this value is confirmed per HPB source and is not zero. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on the closest low-carbohydrate mixed protein salad entry in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Gỏi Bò Tái Chanh exists in any approved source. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (32 × 11.8) / 100 = 3.8 (low). Halal status is unconfirmed as fish sauce brand and beef sourcing are not specified in the HPB record.
